result:
The first thing that arrives is the smell of rain on hot asphalt, a scent that yanks me back to seven years old, barefoot on the cracked sidewalk outside my grandmother’s house in South Carolina, believing the hissing steam was the sound of the planet breathing. I didn’t know the word petrichor; I just knew the world had flipped a switch and suddenly everything alive—ants, azaleas, my own skinny knees—felt rinsed and dangerous.  

I carry that smell around like a secret passport. Whenever I catch it in foreign cities—once in Lisbon, once in a nowhere town in Hokkaido—I’m instantly bilingual in a language whose only words are wonder and ache. The past is a chemical. It leaks through time’s supposedly watertight compartments and hijacks the bloodstream before you can utter the first sentence of caution.  

Right now, in this rented room above a closed-down jazz club, the radiator clanks like a bad drummer trying to find the beat. No rain outside, only the dry cough of winter in Berlin. But the radiator’s hiss is close enough to that childhood steam that I’ve been dislocated, teleported, turned inside out. I’m simultaneously forty-two and seven, inked and aching and still terrified of thunder.  

I keep thinking about how we’re all just walking weather stations, barometers for storms that may have dissolved years ago. A lover raises an eyebrow in a certain way and the humidity jumps thirty percent; suddenly I’m twelve again, in a school corridor, being told I’m forgettable. The body remembers what the storyline refuses to archive.  

There’s a crack in the wall shaped like the Iberian Peninsula. I trace it with my finger, wondering if somewhere in Andalucía a construction worker is tracing a fissure that looks like Berlin. We’re all symmetry, broken and reassembled by strangers who will never meet.  

I used to believe narrative was linear: exposition, rising action, climax, denouement, curtains, applause. Now I think life is a series of jump cuts edited by an overcaffeinated god. Scene: I’m kissing a woman in a cherry orchard. Cut: I’m alone in an emergency room while a doctor uses the word inoperable. Cut: I’m laughing so hard beer comes out of my nose in a Barcelona tapas bar. Cut: the same nose is bleeding onto a police report. Cut: I’m here, watching the Iberian Peninsula sweat plaster dust onto the parquet.  

Memory is not a faithful dog; it’s a feral cat that brings you dead things and expects gratitude. I keep trying to catalog the carcasses: the yellow bicycle stolen when I was nine; the smell of gunmetal after my first hunting trip; the exact slant of light the morning my father cried in the kitchen. But taxonomy fails. The cat keeps dropping new corpses on the pillow while I sleep.  

I read yesterday that the universe is expanding at 74.3 kilometers per second per megaparsec. That means the gap between everything alive is widening faster than any apology can travel. I picture every hurt I’ve caused racing outward like a dishonest galaxy, red-shifting into darkness before it can boomerang back with consequence. And still, somehow, the radiator hisses forgiveness in a language older than any sin I’ve invented.  

There are 936 words left and I’m terrified I’ll waste them on the wrong prophecy. I want to warn the seven-year-old I once was: don’t trust the asphalt steam, it’s a gateway drug to nostalgia. But he wouldn’t listen; he was too busy tasting the edge of the world where the curb met the sky.  

What I really want is to write an encyclopedia of almosts. The job I didn’t take in Nairobi. The baby that didn’t survive the twelfth week. The poem I abandoned after line four, which might have cured someone’s insomnia or started a war. Every almost is a ghost apartment in the skyscraper of you; you keep climbing past those floors, but sometimes, late at night, you hear music behind their locked doors.  

Berlin keeps trying to teach me subtraction. A wall was here, now it’s not. A synagogue burned, now it’s a playground. My own reflection in the window superimposes the 1989 skyline over tonight’s, and for a second I’m a transparent sandwich of histories, bite-marked by time zones.  

Outside, a woman is singing “Blue Moon” in German, off-key but urgent, as if the lyrics are a lifeboat. I open the sash to listen, and cold air barges in like a cop checking for contraband. The singer looks up, startled mid-phrase. Our eyes perform a brief, clumsy choreography: recognition, embarrassment, defiance, retreat. She finishes the song to the pavement, then walks away, coat flapping like a torn flag. I will never know her name, but her voice just became another aisle in my private hardware store; someday I’ll walk down it looking for the screw that fixes a leaking afternoon.  

The word count keeps shrinking, a reverse hourglass. I feel the pressure to land this plane on some meaningful tarmac, but all I have is cabin pressure and a vague dread of arrival. Maybe that’s the honest ending: no summary, no moral, just the slow realization that the flight map was drawn in disappearing ink and we’re all circling the same airport with a name we can’t pronounce.  

 radiator sighs, water cooling in its iron veins. The Iberian Peninsula has gone dark; the streetlights downstairs blink twice, then settle into their orange insomnia. Somewhere in the city a tram grazes a track switch, sparking a brief constellation that nobody catalogs.  

I’m almost out of words. The seven-year-old is asleep on the carpet, cheeks peppered with asphalt freckles. I tuck the remaining syllables under his shoulder, a borrowed blanket against the coming cold. Tomorrow he’ll wake and forget this entire century. I’ll wake and remember the smell of rain that isn’t here, the song I didn’t understand, the crack in the wall migrating toward Africa.  

Last word. Last breath of steam. Last chance to be faithful. I write it: Stay.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
